title: Global incidence and risk factors for glaucoma: A systematic review and meta-analysis of prospective studies
authors: Shan S, Wu J, Cao J, Song P, Rudan I
population: Adults aged 40–79 years across global regions
abstract: This systematic review and meta-analysis estimated the global incidence of glaucoma subtypes from prospective studies. In 2022, the global incidence rate of primary open-angle glaucoma (POAG) was 23.46 (95% CI 15.68-32.91) per 10,000 person-years among those aged 40-79 years, rising from 5.51 per 10,000 at 40-44 years to 64.36 per 10,000 at 75-79 years. The pooled annual cumulative incidence of POAG was 0.21%. Significant disparities existed across geographic regions and age groups, with the highest rates in low sociodemographic index regions and Africa. Six risk factors for POAG were identified, including elevated intraocular pressure, family history, myopia and advanced age.

title: Primary Open Angle Glaucoma Prevalence in Europe: A Systematic Review and Meta-Analysis
authors: Gallo Afflitto G, Aiello F, Cesareo M, Nucci C
population: European adults, predominantly older populations
abstract: This systematic review and meta-analysis evaluated the prevalence of POAG in Europe. From 9348 eligible papers, 10 were included, comprising 26,993 patients. A total of 755 POAG cases were identified, with a pooled prevalence estimate in Europe of 2.60% (95% CI 1.90%-3.56%). Increasing odds for POAG were related to increasing age, with some geographic variation across the continent. POAG prevalence is expected to grow in Europe because of progressively ageing populations.

title: Prevalence and Severity of Glaucoma in the California Medicare Population
authors: Tseng VL, Kitayama K, Yu F, Coleman AL
population: California Medicare beneficiaries aged 65 and over
abstract: This retrospective cross-sectional study examined the prevalence of glaucoma by type and severity in the 2019 California Medicare population. Of 5,856,491 beneficiaries, 220,662 (3.8%) had any glaucoma, 171,988 (2.9%) had POAG. The largest proportion of beneficiaries had moderate to severe glaucoma (31.0% for any glaucoma at moderate stage). Multivariable predictors included older age, Black versus non-Hispanic White race, and higher comorbidity. Beneficiaries with glaucoma had a high prevalence of moderate to severe disease, indicating a need for improved early screening and diagnosis.

title: Twelve-Year Incidence of Open-angle Glaucoma: The Thessaloniki Eye Study
authors: Founti P, Coleman AL, Wilson MR, Topouzis F
population: Elderly White population aged 60 and over, urban Northern Greece
abstract: This longitudinal, population-based study determined the 12-year incidence of open-angle glaucoma (OAG) in an elderly White population in urban Northern Greece. Of 1092 examined subjects, the 12-year incidence of OAG was 4.4% (95% CI 3.3-5.8), approximately 0.37% per year. Incidence of POAG and pseudoexfoliative glaucoma was 2.1% and 2.3% respectively. Pseudoexfoliation was strongly associated with higher odds for incident glaucoma. Only 11.1% of all incident OAG cases had baseline intraocular pressure above 21 mmHg.

title: Socioeconomic and Racial Disparities in Primary Open Angle Glaucoma in the United States
authors: Downs CS, Percelay PJ, Williams B, Mandal N
population: POAG patients at a tertiary care center, Memphis TN
abstract: This study analysed glaucoma severity by demographic factors among 2913 POAG patients in Memphis, TN. Glaucoma severity increased with poverty rate, age, male sex, and Black race. Severity was decreased in patients with private insurance compared with Medicare. The findings indicate that worsening glaucoma severity was associated with higher poverty rates, though isolating socioeconomic status as an independent factor remains challenging given the strong correlation between race and socioeconomic status.

title: Screening for Open-Angle Glaucoma and Its Effect on Blindness
authors: Aspberg J, Heijl A, Bengtsson B
population: Population-based screening cohort, Malmo, Sweden
abstract: This study evaluated the effect of population screening on low vision and blindness from open-angle glaucoma in Malmo, Sweden. Of 42,497 invited subjects, 32,918 were screened. The cumulative incidence of blindness was 0.17% in the screened population versus 0.32% among potential participants, and for low vision 0.25% versus 0.53%. Population screening may reduce bilateral low vision and blindness caused by glaucoma by approximately 50%.

title: Management of open-angle glaucoma by primary eye-care practitioners: toward a personalised medicine approach
authors: Phu J, Agar A, Wang H, Kalloniatis M
population: Patients with early primary open-angle glaucoma
abstract: This review presents recent evidence on treatment modalities for early primary open-angle glaucoma and proposes an updated management framework. Glaucoma is the leading cause of irreversible blindness worldwide, presenting a significant burden to the individual, healthcare provider and healthcare system. Current strategies focus on lowering intraocular pressure via topical drugs, laser trabeculoplasty, filtration surgery or cyclodestructive techniques. Over the last 10 years there has been a significant shift in preferred first-line therapies, particularly early in the disease course.

title: Myopic Open-angle Glaucoma Prevalence in Northeast Asia: A Systematic Review and Meta-analysis of Population-based Studies
authors: Jeong Y, Ha A, Shim SR, Kim YK
population: Northeast Asian general and myopic populations
abstract: This systematic review and meta-analysis investigated myopic open-angle glaucoma prevalence in Northeast Asia across five population-based studies in four countries (12,830 individuals including 7,723 with myopia and 1,112 with OAG). In a myopic population, OAG prevalence was 4.10% (95% CI 3.00-5.70); in a general population, myopic OAG prevalence was 1.10% (95% CI 0.60-1.70). The findings provide a regional prevalence estimate to inform glaucoma studies and public health guidelines for Northeast Asian populations, contributing diagnosed-prevalence context for major Asian markets.

title: Glaucoma: now and beyond
authors: Jayaram H, Kolko M, Friedman DS, Gazzard G
population: Patients across the glaucoma disease spectrum
abstract: The glaucomas are a group of conditions leading to irreversible sight loss, characterised by progressive loss of retinal ganglion cells. Intraocular pressure is the only modifiable risk factor demonstrated by large clinical trials, and remains the leading cause of irreversible blindness. Timely treatment to lower intraocular pressure is effective at slowing vision loss. Many cases remain undiagnosed until advanced stages due to limitations of screening and poor access to case finding. Methods for lowering intraocular pressure include laser treatments, topical medications, and surgery.

title: Evaluating Primary Treatment for People with Advanced Glaucoma: Five-Year Results of the Treatment of Advanced Glaucoma Study
authors: King AJ, Hudson J, Azuara-Blanco A, MacLennan G
population: Adults with newly diagnosed advanced open-angle glaucoma
abstract: The Treatment of Advanced Glaucoma Study (TAGS) was a multicentre randomised controlled trial in 453 adults with newly diagnosed advanced open-angle glaucoma in the UK. At 5 years, vision-specific quality of life measured with the VFQ-25 was similar between primary trabeculectomy (83.3) and medical management (81.3). Mean intraocular pressure was lower with trabeculectomy (12.07 versus 14.76 mmHg), and visual field mean deviation was better preserved. Primary trabeculectomy surgery is more effective in lowering IOP and preventing disease progression than primary medical treatment in patients with advanced disease.

title: Advanced glaucoma at diagnosis: current perspectives
authors: Kastner A, King AJ
population: Patients presenting with advanced glaucoma at diagnosis
abstract: Presentation with advanced glaucoma is a significant risk factor for lifetime blindness. The asymptomatic nature of glaucoma contributes to late presentation. Those most at risk of presenting with advanced glaucoma have asymptomatic high intraocular pressure, no family history, are socially disadvantaged, and do not attend sight testing. Patients with glaucoma may have impaired functionality for daily activities such as reading, walking and driving. Quality of life measures are significantly worse in patients with more severe visual field loss, particularly if bilateral, and decreases faster with further bilateral field loss when advanced damage is present. Management requires disproportionally more resources than earlier disease.

title: Impact of moderate and severe primary open-angle glaucoma on quality of life due to activity limitation
authors: Nayyar S, Kumar S, Rehman O, Ichhpujani P, Singla E
population: Patients with moderate/severe POAG and controls
abstract: This cross-sectional study assessed the impact of moderate and severe POAG on quality of life due to activity limitation, using three glaucoma-specific instruments (GAL-9, GQL-15, Viswanathan) in 122 participants. Quality of life scores worsened progressively from controls to moderate to severe POAG across all instruments. Activity limitation pertaining to dark adaptation affected QoL most in moderate and severe POAG. As disease progressed to severe, limitation of activities requiring central and near vision caused the most significant worsening in QoL. Moderate and severe glaucoma have a clear negative impact on quality of life.

title: Longitudinal evaluation of advanced glaucoma: ten year follow-up cohort study
authors: Shin YI, Jeong Y, Kim YK, Park KH, Jeoung JW
population: Patients with advanced open-angle glaucoma
abstract: This study followed 127 patients with advanced open-angle glaucoma for seven years or more to identify key monitoring factors. Over 11 years, glaucoma progression was detected in 46.5% of eyes. The rate of mean deviation change was -0.43 dB/year overall, -0.67 dB/year for progressors. Inadequate intraocular pressure reduction, better baseline mean deviation, presence of disc haemorrhage, and lower central corneal thickness were identified as risk factors for progression. Patients with higher percentage IOP reduction (greater than 20.94%) had lower risk of progression.

title: Cost-Utility Analysis of Glaucoma Medication Adherence
authors: Newman-Casey PA, Salman M, Lee PP, Gatwood JD
population: Patients with glaucoma aged 40 and over
abstract: This cost-utility analysis estimated the value of adherence to prescribed glaucoma medication using Monte Carlo microsimulation. The majority of patients with glaucoma do not take medications as prescribed. Nonadherent patients proceeded to single-eye blindness as early as 19 years versus 23 years for adherent patients. Total healthcare costs averaged 62,782 dollars for adherent and 52,722 dollars for nonadherent patients, with nonadherent patients having a mean loss of 0.34 quality-adjusted life years, giving a cost-effectiveness ratio of 29,600 dollars per QALY gained. There is room to expand services to improve adherence.

title: Visual Outcomes and Risk Factors for Progression in Juvenile Open-Angle Glaucoma
authors: Seresirikachorn K, Vu DM, Peter Chang TC
population: Patients with juvenile open-angle glaucoma
abstract: This retrospective cohort study reported visual outcomes in 203 eyes of 106 patients with juvenile open-angle glaucoma over 13 years across two tertiary hospitals. At initial assessment, 31.5% of eyes were blind, increasing to 35.5% after nearly 8 years follow-up. Bilateral blindness rose from 15.2% to 19.8%. A higher number of glaucoma surgeries per patient was associated with visual impairment progression. Juvenile open-angle glaucoma is associated with severe visual impairment both at presentation and after treatment, underscoring the need for lifelong follow-up and early detection.

title: Minimally Invasive Glaucoma Surgical Techniques for Open-Angle Glaucoma: An Overview of Cochrane Systematic Reviews and Network Meta-analysis
authors: Bicket AK, Le JT, Azuara-Blanco A, Li T
population: Open-angle glaucoma patients eligible for MIGS
abstract: This overview summarised data from randomised trials of minimally invasive glaucoma surgery (MIGS) for open-angle glaucoma across six Cochrane reviews. Glaucoma affects more than 75 million people worldwide and IOP-lowering surgery is an important treatment. Interest in reducing surgical morbidity has led to introduction of MIGS. Adding a Hydrus or iStent microstent to cataract surgery safely improved the likelihood of drop-free glaucoma control compared with cataract surgery alone. Trabecular bypass devices including iStent improved drop-free disease control, supporting MIGS as an option for eligible patients.

title: Minimally Invasive Glaucoma Surgery
authors: Voykov B, Prokosch V, Lubke J
population: German adults aged 35–74 with glaucoma
abstract: Approximately 1.4% of the German population aged 35 to 74 suffers from glaucoma, one of the more common causes of blindness. The only evidence-based treatment is lowering intraocular pressure. Trabeculectomy is the reference standard in glaucoma surgery but leads to complications in 3-15% of cases. In minimally invasive glaucoma surgery, very small stents can be implanted with a better safety profile, indicated for mild or moderate glaucoma. The iStent inject and Hydrus Microstent procedures demonstrated additional pressure-lowering effect beyond cataract surgery (1.9 and 1.8 mmHg) and have been found to slow the progression of visual field defects.

title: Is minimally invasive glaucoma surgery killing trabeculectomies?
authors: Loon SC, Ang BCH, White A
population: Glaucoma patients undergoing surgical intervention
abstract: The introduction of minimally invasive glaucoma surgery (MIGS) has changed the surgical landscape of glaucoma. For more than four decades the most commonly performed glaucoma surgery has been trabeculectomy. There is a growing trend of mild to moderate glaucoma cases undergoing MIGS procedures instead of trabeculectomy. Glaucoma drainage device implantation may also have taken a significant share of filtering surgery for more severe glaucoma. Currently trabeculectomy is still the most common glaucoma surgery performed worldwide, however the proportion undergoing MIGS is increasing rapidly.

title: Visual Field Outcomes in the Primary Tube Versus Trabeculectomy Study
authors: Swaminathan SS, Jammal AA, Medeiros FA, Gedde SJ
population: Eyes with uncontrolled glaucoma undergoing primary surgery
abstract: This cohort analysis of the Primary Tube Versus Trabeculectomy Study described visual field outcomes in 155 eyes randomised to tube shunt surgery or trabeculectomy with mitomycin C. The mean rate of change in standard automated perimetry mean deviation was -0.32 dB/year in the trabeculectomy group and -0.47 dB/year in the tube group, with no statistically significant difference. Worse intraocular pressure control was significantly associated with faster rates of mean deviation loss, and older patients were at risk for faster progression, underscoring the importance of IOP control in surgically managed patients.

title: Ab-Externo MicroShunt versus Trabeculectomy in Primary Open-Angle Glaucoma: Two-Year Results from a Randomized, Multicenter Study
authors: Panarelli JF, Moster MR, Garcia-Feijoo J, Khaw PT
population: Adults with POAG inadequately controlled on maximum tolerated medical therapy
abstract: This prospective randomised multicentre trial compared the MicroShunt versus trabeculectomy in adults with mild to severe POAG inadequately controlled on maximum tolerated medical therapy with IOP at or above 15 mmHg. At 2 years, surgical success was lower in the MicroShunt group than trabeculectomy (50.6% versus 64.4%). Mean diurnal IOP was reduced from 21.1 to 13.9 mmHg in the MicroShunt group and from 21.1 to 10.7 mmHg in the trabeculectomy group. Both interventions provided significant reductions in IOP and medication use, with trabeculectomy having greater surgical success. This characterises the inadequately-controlled population requiring surgical escalation.

title: PreserFlo MicroShunt versus trabeculectomy: an updated meta-analysis and systematic review
authors: Governatori L, Oliverio L, Mermoud A, Scampoli A
journal: Graefe's Archive for Clinical and Experimental Ophthalmology
population: Patients with moderate-to-advanced glaucoma
abstract: This meta-analysis compared PreserFlo MicroShunt and trabeculectomy in moderate-to-advanced glaucoma (mean deviation worse than -6 dB) across 10 studies and 1833 eyes. Preoperative IOP was 22.1 mmHg and 56.1% had moderate-to-severe glaucoma. No significant differences were found in qualified and complete success rates. Trabeculectomy achieved better IOP outcomes and greater IOP reduction, while PreserFlo showed less visual field progression and lower hypotony risk. The analysis characterises surgical decision-making in the uncontrolled, surgically-eligible glaucoma population.

title: Ab interno supraciliary microstent surgery for open-angle glaucoma
authors: Sandhu A, Jayaram H, Hu K, Gazzard G
population: Open-angle glaucoma patients eligible for microstent surgery
abstract: This Cochrane review evaluated supraciliary microstent surgery for open-angle glaucoma. Glaucoma is the leading cause of global irreversible blindness, often associated with raised intraocular pressure. Where medical or laser treatment has failed or is not tolerated, surgery is often required, and minimally-invasive surgical approaches have been developed to reduce IOP with lower surgical risks. One trial of 505 eyes showed supraciliary microstent combined with phacoemulsification increased the proportion of medication-free patients at 24 months (85% versus 59%) with greater IOP reduction, though long-term safety concerns led to device withdrawal. Few high-quality studies compare MIGS to standard treatments.
